author: Augustine of Hippo
book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
Sin is Only from the Will. His Own Life and Will Best Known to Each Individual. What Will is.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Sin is Only from the Will. His Own Life and Will Best Known to Each Individual. What Will is.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For let any men whatever, if only no madness has broken them loose from the common sense of the human race, bring whatever zeal they like for judging, whatever ignorance, nay whatever slowness of mind, I should like to find out what they would have replied to me had I asked, whether a man would seem to them to have sinned by whose hand while he was asleep another should have written something disgraceful?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Who doubts that they would have denied that it is a sin, and have exclaimed against it so vehemently that they might perchance have been enraged that I should have thought them proper objects of such a question?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Of whom reconciled and restored to equanimity, as best I could do it, I should have begged that they would not take it amiss if I asked them another thing just as manifest, just as completely within the knowledge of all.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Then I should have asked, if some stronger person had done some evil thing by the hand of one not sleeping but conscious, yet with the rest of his members bound and in constraint, whether because he knew it, though absolutely unwilling, he should be held guilty of any sin?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: And here all marvelling that I should ask such questions, would reply without hesitation, that he had absolutely not sinned at all.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Because whoever has done anything evil by means of one unconscious or unable to resist, the latter can by no means be justly condemned.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: And precisely why this is so, if I should inquire of the human nature in these men, I should easily bring out the desired answer, by asking in this manner: Suppose that the sleeper already knew what the other would do with his hand, and of purpose aforethought, having drunk so much as would prevent his being awakened, should go to sleep, in order to deceive some one with an oath.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Would any amount of sleep suffice to prove his innocence?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: What else than a guilty man would one pronounce him?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if he has also willingly been bound that he may deceive some one by this pretext, in what respect then would those chains profit as a means of relieving him of sin?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Although bound by these he was really not able to resist, as in the other case the sleeper was absolutely ignorant of what he was then doing.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Is there therefore any possibility of doubting that both should be judged to have sinned?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Which things having been conceded, I should have argued, that sin is indeed nowhere but in the will, since this consideration also would have helped me, that justice holds guilty those sinning by evil will alone, although they may have been unable to accomplish what they willed.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For who could have said that, in adducing these considerations, I was dwelling upon obscure and recondite things, where on account of the fewness of those able to understand, either fraud or suspicion of ostentation is accustomed to arise?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Let that distinction between intelligible and sensible things withdraw for a little: let me not be found fault with for following up slow minds with the stimuli of subtle disputations.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Permit me to know that I live, permit me to know that I will to live.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If in this the human race agrees, as our life is known to us, so also is our will.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Nor when we become possessed of this knowledge, is there any occasion to fear lest any one should convince us that we may be deceived; for no one can be deceived as to whether he does not live, or wishes nothing.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: I do not think that I have adduced anything obscure, and my concern is rather lest some should find fault with me for dwelling on things that are too manifest.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But let us consider the bearing of these things.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Sinning therefore takes place only by exercise of will.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But our will is very well known to us; for neither should I know that I will, if I did not know what will itself is.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Accordingly, it is thus defined: will is a movement of mind, no one compelling, either for not losing or for obtaining something.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Why therefore could not I have so defined it then?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Was it difficult to see that one unwilling is contrary to one willing, just as the left hand is contrary to the right, not as black to white?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For the same thing cannot be at the same time black and white.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But whoever is placed between two men is on the left hand with reference to one, on the right with reference to the other.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: One man is both on the right hand and on the left hand at the same time, but by no means both to the one man.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: So indeed one mind may be at the same time unwilling and willing, but it cannot be at the same time unwilling and willing with reference to one and the same thing.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For when any one unwillingly does anything; if you ask him whether he wished to do it, he says that he did not.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Likewise if you ask whether he wished not to do it, he replies that he did.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: So you will find him unwilling with reference to doing, willing with reference to not doing, that is to say, one mind at the same time having both attitudes, but each referring to different things.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Why do I say this?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Because if we should again ask wherefore though unwilling he does this, he will say that he is compelled.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For every one also who does a thing unwillingly is compelled, and every one who is compelled, if he does a thing, does it only unwillingly.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: It follows that he that is willing is free from compulsion, even if any one thinks himself compelled.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: And in this manner every one who willingly does a thing is not compelled, and whoever is not compelled, either does it willingly or not at all.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Since nature itself proclaims these things in all men whom we can interrogate without absurdity, from the boy even to the old man, from literary sport even to the throne of the wise, why then should I not have seen that in the definition of will should be put, "no one compelling," which now as if with greater experience most cautiously I have done.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if this is everywhere manifest, and promptly occurs to all not by instruction but by nature, what is there left that seems obscure, unless perchance it be concealed from some one, that when we wish for something, we will, and our mind is moved towards it, and we either have it or do not have it, and if we have it we will to retain it, if we have it not, to acquire it?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Wherefore everyone who wills, wills either not to lose something or to obtain it.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Hence if all these things are clearer than day, as they are, nor are they given to my conception alone, but by the liberality of truth itself to the whole human race, why could I not have said even at that time: Will is a movement of the mind, no one compelling, either for not losing or for obtaining something?
